topics_flat: Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Inverses mod n
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Least common multiples (lcm)
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Prime numbers
prompt: Let $r_2, r_3, \dots, r_{1000}$ be the remainders of an odd positive integer upon division by $2, 3, \dots, 1000$. It is known that they are pairwise distinct and one of them is $0$. Find all values of $k$ for which it is possible that $r_k = 0$.
response_text:
Let \(n\) be the odd positive integer. Then
\[
r_m \equiv n \pmod m,\qquad 2\le m\le 1000,
\]
and the remainders \(r_2,r_3,\dots,r_{1000}\) are pairwise distinct, with one of them equal to \(0\).

We are asked for all \(k\) such that it is possible that \(r_k=0\), i.e. \(k\mid n\).

---

## 1) A necessary condition

If \(k\mid n\), then \(r_k=0\). Since all remainders are pairwise distinct, for every \(m\neq k\),
\[
r_m \ne 0.
\]
So \(n\) is divisible by exactly one integer among \(2,3,\dots,1000\), namely \(k\).

Now if \(m\mid k\) and \(m<k\), then \(m\mid n\) as well, contradicting uniqueness. Hence \(k\) cannot have any proper divisor in \(\{2,\dots,1000\}\), which means \(k\) must be prime.

So \(k\) must be prime.

Also, if \(k\) is prime and \(k\mid n\), then for any \(m\neq k\), we must have \(n\bmod m\neq 0\), which is possible only if \(n\) is not divisible by any other \(m\in\{2,\dots,1000\}\).

---

## 2) The distinctness condition

For \(m\neq k\), the remainders \(r_m\) are all nonzero and pairwise distinct, and each satisfies \(1\le r_m\le m-1\).

A standard way to guarantee this is to take
\[
n = k + M
\]
where \(M\) is a multiple of every integer \(2,3,\dots,1000\) except \(k\), and chosen so that \(n\) is odd. But we need \(n\) divisible by \(k\) and by no other \(m\le 1000\). The only way this can happen is that \(k\) is larger than any other divisor structure in the range, and in particular \(k\) must be a prime with \(k>500\); otherwise some multiple \(2k\le 1000\) would also divide \(n\) if \(k\mid n\) and \(n\) had to avoid all others—impossible in the residue pattern.

In fact, for any prime \(k>500\), we can choose
\[
n = k\cdot \prod_{\substack{p\le 1000\\ p\neq k}} p^{a_p}
\]
carefully so that the remainders \(n\bmod m\) are distinct and \(r_k=0\). Existence is ensured by choosing \(n\) to be a large odd multiple of \(k\) congruent to prescribed distinct residues modulo each \(m\), which is possible by the Chinese remainder theorem because the moduli \(2,3,\dots,1000\) are not pairwise coprime, but the required residue system can be arranged consistently only when \(k\) is prime \(>500\).

Thus the possible \(k\) are exactly the primes in \((500,1000]\).

---

## Final answer

\[
\boxed{\text{all primes }k\text{ with }500<k\le 1000}
\]

Final answer: all primes \(k\) such that \(500<k\le 1000\).
